title: SLO Burn Rate and Error Budget Tracking
dataSources: SLO compliance metrics, error budget calculations
app: ITSI, custom SLO metrics, APM data
spl:
index=slos sourcetype="slo:compliance"
| eval burn_rate=1-(success_count/(success_count+failure_count))
| bin _time span=1h
| stats avg(burn_rate) as avg_burn, sum(error_budget_consumed) as consumed by service, slo_name, _time
| where avg_burn > 0.1
description: Burn rate and error budget show how fast SLO is consumed. Tracking supports proactive action before SLA breach and prioritization of reliability work.
value: Burn rate and error budget show how fast SLO is consumed. Tracking supports proactive action before SLA breach and prioritization of reliability work.
implementation: Compute SLO compliance and error budget from availability/latency data. Ingest into Splunk. Alert on burn rate above threshold or error budget exhaustion. Report on remaining budget by service.
visualization: Gauge (error budget remaining), Line chart (burn rate), Table (services by budget consumed).
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: ITSI, custom SLO metrics, APM data.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: SLO compliance metrics, error budget calculations.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Compute SLO compliance and error budget from availability/latency data. Ingest into Splunk. Alert on burn rate above threshold or error budget exhaustion. Report on remaining budget by service.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=slos sourcetype="slo:compliance"
| eval burn_rate=1-(success_count/(success_count+failure_count))
| bin _time span=1h
| stats avg(burn_rate) as avg_burn, sum(error_budget_consumed) as consumed by service, slo_name, _time
| where avg_burn > 0.1
```

Understanding this SPL

**SLO Burn Rate and Error Budget Tracking** — Burn rate and error budget show how fast SLO is consumed. Tracking supports proactive action before SLA breach and prioritization of reliability work.

Documented **Data sources**: SLO compliance metrics, error budget calculations. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): ITSI, custom SLO metrics, APM data. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: slos; **sourcetype**: slo:compliance. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=slos, sourcetype="slo:compliance". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **burn_rate** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Discretizes time or numeric ranges with `bin`/`bucket`.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by service, slo_name, _time** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Filters the current rows with `where avg_burn > 0.1` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.


Step 3 — Validate
Run the search for a time range you can explain. Compare with the closest Grafana dashboard, APM UI, or Splunk monitoring console, and spot-check a few raw rows for fields that matter.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Gauge (error budget remaining), Line chart (burn rate), Table (services by budget consumed).
grandmaExplanation: We see how fast we are spending room for mistakes so we can act before a small issue eats the month.
knownFalsePositives: Latency spikes during pool exhaustion, garbage collection, or upstream slowness. We compare with the APM UI and deploy history before a code-only theory.
